segments.text: Now we come to another Sutta, 4.20.198, the Buddha said,
monks, these four persons are found existing in the world.
Here in monks, a certain person is a self-tomenter,
given to the practice of self-toment.
Here in again, a certain person is a tormenter of another,
given to the practice of tormenting another.
Yet again, a certain person is a tormenter, both of self and another,
Yet again, monks, a certain person is a tormenter,
neither of self nor of another,
nor given to the practice of tormenting self and another.
He who torments neither self nor another in this very life is no more hungry,
he is a lay, he has become cool,
who dwells with self that has become Brahma.
And how monks is a person a self-tomenter,
one given to the practice of self-toment.
In this case, a certain one goes naked,
he has loose habits, he licks his hands clean,
he will have none of your come in your reverence,
or stop a bit your reverence.
He refuses food brought to him,
he refuses an invitation to a meal,
he refuses food straight from the pot or straight from the pan,
or within the threshold of a door,
or among the firewood, or among the rice pounders.
He refuses food when a couple are eating,
or from a woman giving suck,
or from one sleeping with a man.
He refuses food from a mixed collection,
or where a dog stands by,
he eats neither fish nor flesh,

He comes to know, destroyed His rebirth, lived His holy life.
Done is what was to be done. There is no coming to the state of things.
Thus, monks, a person is neither a tormentor of self, nor of another,
nor given to the practice thereof.
He being neither of these in this very life, hungers no more.
He abides in the experience of bliss with the self that has become Brahma.
So, monks, these are the four persons found existing in the world.
That's the end of the Sutta.
So, here, in this last part of the Sutta, you find several factors explained.
The first one is Sela. The second one is contentment.
The third one is guarding the sense doors, then mindfulness and awareness.
Then seclusion, then abandonment of the hindrances, then attainment of the four jhanas.
These factors, grouped together, are called Charana, sometimes translated as conduct.
One of the names of the Buddha is Vijacharana Sampanno,
replete or complete in knowledge and conduct.
So, this conduct is the holy path, which is the practice of the holy path.
Now, in this Sutta, these factors that I have just read to you just now,
three factors are not in here.
These, in other words, there are seven factors here mentioned, seven factors.
Three are not mentioned, and the three that are not mentioned is got from other Suttas.
The first one is moderation in eating, bojane, matanyuta.
The second one is devoted to wakefulness, jaggarhya, nuyoga.
The third one is the seventh, true dhammas, saddhamma,
faith, conscience, remorse, much-learning, energetic effort, mindfulness, wisdom.
Now, the famous Lady Saidore in his books, he described this Charana Conduct.
He also took these seven factors from the Diga Nikhaya.
The factors given in the Diga Nikhaya are just the seven mentioned here.
And he missed out these other three.
These other three are found in other Suttas, like Majima Nikhaya.
And also Angutra Nikhaya, like earlier I read that if a monk possesses three things,
And these two factors were mentioned there, moderation in eating and devoted to wakefulness.
And these seven saddhammas are found in the Majima Nikhaya.
So, if you take these seven, the complete factors for Charana Conduct or practice is actually the seven which are found here.
Namely, sila, contentment, guarding the sense doors, mindfulness and awareness, seclusion, abandonment of the hindrances,
and attaining of the four jhanas.
Then you add another three, moderation in eating, devotion to wakefulness, and the seven true dhammas, seven saddhammas.
The other thing I'd like to mention here, at the end of this Sutta is described the ending of the asavas.
And the asavas mentioned here is the asava of sense desire, sensual desire, kama-sava,
then the asava of becoming, then the asava of ignorance.
So, here there are only three things I've mentioned as asavas.
And then in some later suttas, for example, in some other suttas, they mentioned four.
And they mentioned four plus another one, I think on views.
